lines.text: Nick Trulino here. We're {{ELAPSED}} into this thing and the numbers are *bad*, folks. Kill count's up double digits from last year at this hour. So let's do what we do — we play the records that saved our lives when we were seventeen and didn't know better. Green Day. 'Basket Case.' 1994.

Billie Joe Armstrong. Ten years old when his dad died. Mike Dirnt — heroin-addicted mother, adopted by a Native American mother and white father who split when he was seven. These guys built a sound out of *trauma*, man. That's punk.

Dookie. Recorded in three weeks. September and October 1993 at Fantasy Studios in Berkeley. Rob Cavallo producing. They spent more time deciding which coffee to order than tracking drums. And it *still* sold twenty million copies worldwide. Twenty *million*.
Armstrong's lyrics — boredom, anxiety, mass murder, divorce, masturbation. Real stuff. Not the sanitized version. The version that makes you feel less alone at midnight. Here's 'Basket Case.'

NOFX. 'Linoleum.' 1996. These guys formed in LA in '83 and spent the next forty years refusing to sign to a major label despite having the kind of staying power that should have made them MTV. They didn't *want* MTV.

Fat Mike. Erik Sandin. Eric Melvin. El Hefe on lead guitar and trumpet starting '91. The name came from a Boston hardcore band called Negative FX. They took that *prefix* and made an empire out of it. Beautiful, right?

Punk in Drublic. 1994. Number twelve on Heatseekers. Gold status six years later. And they *still* didn't break. Refused to send the 'Leave It Alone' video to MTV. Fat Mike said — I'm paraphrasing — 'We didn't want to be part of that machine.' That's the Purge ethos right there. Opt out.
So Long and Thanks for All the Shoes in '97. A return to faster punk. These guys understood that speed is philosophy. You move fast, you don't have time to doubt yourself. You just *exist*.
'Linoleum.' You know this one. Play it.
The Descendents. 'Hope.' From Milo Goes to College. 1982. Manhattan Beach, California. A power pop/surf punk band that became something else entirely when they found Milo Aukerman.

Milo actually *went* to college. UC San Diego. While the band was touring. That's not a metaphor — that's literal commitment to the bit. He's in class studying while his bandmates are loading gear into a van.

The Descendents sang about things other bands wouldn't touch. Parents. Food. Girls. Rejection. Stuff that *mattered* to actual humans. Steven Blush said their 'cheeky love songs disguised as hardcore blasts became the most aped formula in rock.' They invented something and then everyone copied it.

Bill Stevenson recorded their farts on the title track. I'm not joking. They had band members cheering each other on while they farted into recording equipment because *that's* punk. No pretense. No filter. Just — here's what we are.
And the song 'Hope.' Listen to it. Really listen. It's about wanting something better and not knowing if you're going to get there. That's {{CLOCK}}. That's midnight on Purge Night. That's us.
Anti-Flag. Pittsburgh. 1988. Justin Sane and Pat Thetic. 'The Inequality of It All.' 2004. This is the song that understood what was coming before we all lived in it.
Anti-war activism. Criticism of US foreign policy. Corporatism. Wealth distribution. That's *their* entire catalog. Not a hobby — a *religion*. Every song is a manifesto.

2003. The Terror State album. Bush Administration. War on terrorism. They were prophetic, man. They saw the machine and they named it. And now we're living in what they warned about — just transposed seventy years forward under a different logo.

Tony Visconti produced one of their albums. *Tony Visconti*. The guy who worked with Bowie. They added keyboards, strings, a children's chorus. Punk rock with orchestration. Why? Because Anti-Flag understood that sometimes the message needs *weight*.
Here's 'The Inequality of It All.' And if this doesn't make you feel something at {{CLOCK}} on a Purge Night, you're not actually listening.
Blink-182. Poway, California. Northern suburb of San Diego. 1992. Mark Hoppus, Tom DeLonge, Scott Raynor. 'Dammit.' 1997. This is the song that made pop-punk a thing.

DeLonge and Raynor met at a Battle of the Bands competition. A *competition*. Most bands get drunk at a party and decide to form. These guys battled their way into existence. Travis Barker joined in '98 and the *real* Blink-182 was born.
Cheshire Cat. Three days. Westbeach Recorders in LA. Three *days*. You know what you can do in three days? A lot less than a perfect album. But they didn't need perfect — they needed *honest*.

Hoppus fell from a lamppost in front of DeLonge's house. Broke his ankles. Three weeks on crutches trying to impress the guy. That's the energy. That's the willingness to *break yourself* for the bit.

Their lyrics — relationships, adolescent frustration, immaturity. Straight lines. No pretense. Pop-punk's second mainstream rise happened because they refused to pretend to be angrier than they were. They were just *seventeen* and stupid and it was *honest*.
'Dammit.' The song that launched a thousand garage bands. Play it.
Dead Milkmen. Philadelphia. 1983. Rodney Anonymous. Joe Jack Talcum. Dave Blood. Dean Clean. 'Punk Rock Girl.' 1988. From Beelzebubba. The song that MTV played and nobody could explain why.

The band started in 1979 as a home-recording project. High school friends making cassettes. The name came from Toni Morrison — Milkman Dead from Song of Solomon. They took a character from *literature* and turned her into a punk anthem.

'Bitchin' Camaro' hit college radio first. 1985. Big Lizard in My Backyard. And then they did something smarter — they made a song that was actually *about something*. 'Punk Rock Girl' is a love song. A real love song. MTV couldn't ignore it.

Their sound — jangly. Sardonic. Thick Philadelphia accents. They sang about toxic waste, jellyfish afterlifes, UFOs, conspiracy theories. The hardcore scene didn't know what to do with humor. They thought punk had to be *serious*. Dead Milkmen proved you could be both.

Club MTV performance. Chaotic. Irreverent. Stage antics. They understood something about punk that the serious guys missed — it's *fun*. It's supposed to be *fun*. You're not saving the world. You're just making noise and finding your people while you do it.

Here at {{CLOCK}}, six hours in, with the kill count running hot — we need this. We need 'Punk Rock Girl.' We need to remember that somewhere under all this, there's supposed to be something that feels good.

tickerItems.text: Cumulative kills (Madison, midnight): 82. Up 21% from this point last year. East Side holding steady at 18.2% of total activity.
State Street Corridor: coordinated group activity reported 23:00-00:30. 14 kills in 90 minutes. Worst single-event sequence of 2168. Security response insufficient.
PurgeShield panels: last-minute installs available through 02:00. Call 608-SHIELD-1. 4,200 units deployed Madison metro last year. Are you protected?
